issue_title: Allow for creating a schema that behaves like Dict field
Could you please clarify the documentation for the following code
issue_body:
```
    def __init__(self, message, field_names=None, fields=None, data=None, **kwargs):
        if not isinstance(message, dict) and not isinstance(message, list):
            messages = [message]
        else:
            messages = message
        #: String, list, or dictionary of error messages.
        #: If a `dict`, the keys will be field names and the values will be lists of
        #: messages.
        self.messages = messages
        #: List of field objects which failed validation.
        self.fields = fields
        if isinstance(field_names, basestring):
            #: List of field_names which failed validation.
            self.field_names = [field_names]
        else:  # fields is a list or None
            self.field_names = field_names or []
        # Store nested data
        self.data = data
        self.kwargs = kwargs
        MarshmallowError.__init__(self, message)
```

I'm not sure why I would want fields and field_names.  Could you clarify with a use case please?

issue_title: Replace references to apply_authorization_limits and apply_limits from cookbook and code
Python documentation of loading Caffe Model Weights is not correct
issue_body: In the documentation https://bigdl-project.github.io/0.2.0/#ProgrammingGuide/caffe-support/, the python example of "Load Caffe Model Weights to Predefined BigDL Model" is not correct, the API is Model.load_caffe(bigdlModel, caffe.prototxt, caffe.model, match_all=True), not Model.load_caffe_model(bigdlModel, caffe.prototxt, caffe.model, match_all=True).

[error]               val localBuffer = BlockManagerWrapper.byteBufferConvert(weightBuffer)
[error]                                                                       ^
[error] /Users/srowen/Documents/Cloudera/BigDL/spark/dl/src/main/scala/com/intel/analytics/bigdl/parameters/AllReduceParameter.scala:229: type mismatch;
[error]  found   : Object
[error]  required: org.apache.spark.util.io.ChunkedByteBuffer
[error]               .getOrElse(bm.getRemoteBytes(blockId).get))
[error]         
```

The signature of `BlockManager.getLocalBytes` changed from Spark 2.1.x to Spark 2.2.0 in https://github.com/apache/spark/commit/b56ad2b1ec19fd60fa9d4926d12244fd3f56aca4 . 

I know that's not how BigDL is built, but it ends up causing problems at runtime. For example, @nishamuktewar noted this happens when you run on CDH 5.12 + Spark 2.2:

```
ERROR optim.DistriOptimizer$: Error: org.apache.spark.SparkException: Job aborted due to stage failure: Task 0 in stage 11.0 failed 4 times, most recent failure: Lost task 0.3 in stage 11.0 (TID 40, bottou02.sjc.cloudera.com, executor 2): java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException: java.lang.ClassCastException: org.apache.spark.storage.ByteBufferBlockData cannot be cast to org.apache.spark.util.io.ChunkedByteBuffer
  at java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.report(FutureTask.java:122)
  at java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.get(FutureTask.java:192)
  at com.intel.analytics.bigdl.parameters.FutureResult$$anonfun$waitResult$1.apply(AllReduceParameter.scala:244)
  at com.intel.analytics.bigdl.parameters.FutureResult$$anonfun$waitResult$1.apply(AllReduceParameter.scala:244)
  at scala.collection.TraversableLike$$anonfun$map$1.apply(TraversableLike.scala:234)
  at scala.collection.TraversableLike$$anonfun$map$1.apply(TraversableLike.scala:234)
  at scala.collection.Iterator$class.foreach(Iterator.scala:893)
  at scala.collection.AbstractIterator.foreach(Iterator.scala:1336)
...
```

I think the fix is just to push this reference to `BlockManager` into `BlockManagerWrapper` and use reflection as needed to patch over the difference.

issue_title: Add unittest back for load model from Bigdl
Best way to combine interaction matrices
issue_body:
Hi,

I'd like to build a website, where users can like items and after they liked an item, they can refine what features of that item they liked. (one item can have max ~50 features)
On top of the above explicit feedback, I'd like to incorporate some implicit feedback, like whether a user clicked on an item, percentage of photos viewed, time spent on the item's profile, etc.

Since LightFM was designed to use only one interaction matrix, I should have somehow combine them. On possible way forward would entail the following:

1. Train a model with implicit click-through data (could be dense)
2. Train a model with explicit item likes (sparse)
3. Train a separate model for each features; same size as the above two matrix; for every feature f the value is 1 if user u liked feature f of item i (sparser)

Now I can call predict on all the above models, and average their results. I can also use weighted average, e.g. models based on explicit likes get higher weights, etc. Also, I have not put much thought into it yet, but it might be possible to learn the weights for the models automatically.

Is there a better/more efficient way to combine the interaction matrices?

Reading through the LightFM paper, Factorisation Machines might suit my needs better, because unlike LightFM, they can solve for multiple interaction matrices.
Therefore, does it make sense to attempt to use LightFM in my situation?

issue_title: use np.expm1 in singlediode model for exp(x) - 1
issue_labels: [{'id': 286475252, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyODY0NzUyNTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/easy', 'name': 'easy', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 177800479, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxNzc4MDA0Nzk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 961364188, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQxODg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '97f4ee', 'default': True}, {'id': 961364003, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQwMDM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '3bcc79', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
**Problem**
it is possible that at extremely small voltages there may be machine precision error of less than 1 part per million because the [single diode model](https://pvpmc.sandia.gov/modeling-steps/2-dc-module-iv/diode-equivalent-circuit-models/) has the term `exp(x) - 1` which has an increasing error as x → 0 because `exp(x)` → 1, so 1 - 1 is a very, very small number close to zero.

**Solution**
Use [`np.expm1(x)`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.expm1.html) instead of `np.exp(x) - 1` as suggested in [this comment](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/pull/409#discussion_r199295338)

**Additional context**
#409

Just had a new version of the test stack deployed via CI/CD.  In the CD deploy step the traffic is switched automatically 100% to the newest version and the oldest one is scheduled for deletion.

Noticed a problem in the newest version, so wanted to switch traffic back to old stable one.  Observed the current traffic report:
```

Issued command to move 100% of the traffic from cd212 back to cd210.  The oldest one, cd209 should not be involved in any way, however:
```
$ senza traffic xxx-test cd210 100

issue_title: C++ api linking error due to missing inline in z3++.h
Parsing datatype sorts using Java API leading to redundant function declarations
issue_body:
Hi, I noticed that if I have in a .z3 file:
```
(declare-datatypes () ((General
    (G_Boolean (of_G_Boolean Bool))
    (G_Integer (of_G_Integer Int))
    (G_String (of_G_String String))
    G_Null
)))

(declare-const _v_ General)
(assert (= (of_G_String _v_) "test"))
```
and try to parse it using context.parseSMTLIB2String (without specifying additional sorts or function declarations) followed by adding it as an assertion to solver, the solver.toString() will return
```
(declare-datatypes () ((General (G_Boolean (of_G_Boolean Bool)) (G_Integer (of_G_Integer Int)) (G_String (of_G_String String)) (G_Null))))
(declare-fun G_Boolean (Bool) General)
(declare-fun of_G_Boolean (General) Bool)
(declare-fun G_Integer (Int) General)
(declare-fun of_G_Integer (General) Int)
(declare-fun G_String (String) General)
(declare-fun of_G_String (General) String)
(declare-fun G_Null () General)
(declare-fun _v_ () General)
(assert (= (of_G_String _v_) "test"))
```

Is this expected? I was expecting only the line 1 and 9-10. In more complex scenarios (with several assertions around the datatype) it ends generating invalid models (that don't respect some assertion), however when I remove those additional function declarations it produces completely correct models.

So, my question is whether it is supposed for the Java API to create those additional function declarations or not.

issue_title: [TarFS] Consider separating out getinfo_from_member(member)
issue_body:
Hi,

In my use case I have 2 requirements:
- walk a tree where *.tar.gz, *.zip and possibly others are treated like directories
- open possibly large tar-files in streaming mode="r|*" and avoid jumping backwards and forwards within that file. This requirement is also the reason why my walker must return a file descriptor (fd) and ResourceInfo

I've implemented a walker that makes heavy use of yours and transparently handles the container files (*.tar.*) when such a file is found. It yields the fd and ResourceInfo exactly as wanted. Except that I had to copy a piece of your code from tarfs.py to create the Info object for tar file entries. Remember I'm opening the tar file myself and I'm (sequentially) iterating through it myself. Yet I still want to consistently return an Info object.

I called my new function getinfo_from_member(member) which is more or less a copy of the 2nd half of getinfo(). Remember that I'm not able to use the tarfs.getinfo() implementation because it "seeks" the information in the tar file, which I'm not able (don't want) to do in streaming mode.

It would be great if I didn't have to copy the source code but call a tarfs method instead. Do think you could separate it out?

thanks a lot
Juergen

issue_title: BaseMoneyValidator fails to validate falsy ints and Decimals
issue_body:
In [the validator code](https://github.com/django-money/django-money/blob/master/djmoney/models/validators.py#L32), any subclass of `BaseMoneyValidator` will short-circuit the validation if the validator was initialised with a falsy integer or or decimal.

Attach a debugger at this code:

    if not limit_value:  # 0 or Decimal('0')
        return

Steps to reproduce:

1. Create a `MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, default_currency='USD')` (copied from example) but with an additional validator, like so:

    ```
    MoneyField(
        max_digits=10, decimal_places=2,
        default_currency='USD',
        validators=[
            MinMoneyValidator(0),
        ]
    )
    ```

1. Make a migration and migrate.
1. Run the server and save any object with that new field with the Money equivalent of `(-1, 'USD')`. Observe that the validator does not raise a `ValidationError`.

This affects the common use case of validating against negative amounts of money.

issue_title: Filter using ForeignKey field raises FieldError
issue_body:
I've found a bug which seems to be a regression in 0.11.1 - everything was fine in 0.11

So I have code such as
```python
import datetime as datetime

class MoneyTest(models.Model):
    date = models.DateTimeField()

class MoneyItemTest(models.Model):
    money =  models.ForeignKey(Purchase, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    price = MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, default_currency='EUR', default=Decimal('0.0'))


# Queries
date = datetime.datetime(year=2017, month=2, day=1)
query = MoneyItemTest.objects.filter(money__date=date) # still works
query = MoneyItemTest.objects.filter(money__date__gt=date) # worked in 0.11, crashes in 0.11.1
```

The second query generates this traceback
```bash
File "\env\lib\site-packages\django\db\models\manager.py", line 85, in manager_method
    return getattr(self.get_queryset(), name)(*args, **kwargs)
File "\env\lib\site-packages\djmoney\models\managers.py", line 211, in wrapper
    args, kwargs = _expand_money_kwargs(model, args, kwargs, exclusions)
File "\env\lib\site-packages\djmoney\models\managers.py", line 150, in _expand_money_kwargs
    field = _get_field(model, name)
File "\env\lib\site-packages\djmoney\models\managers.py", line 55, in _get_field
    return qs.names_to_path(parts, qs.get_meta(), True, fail_on_missing=False)[1]
File "\env\lib\site-packages\django\db\models\sql\query.py", line 1363, in names_to_path
    "Choices are: %s" % (name, ", ".join(available)))
django.core.exceptions.FieldError: Cannot resolve keyword 'money' into field. Choices are: date, id, moneyitemtest
```

If that's of any help, I get these warnings too:
```bash
\env\lib\site-packages\djmoney\models\managers.py:58: RemovedInDjango20Warning: Usage of field.rel has been deprecated. Use field.remote_field instead.
  model = lookup_field.rel.to
\env\lib\site-packages\djmoney\models\managers.py:58: RemovedInDjango20Warning: Usage of ForeignObjectRel.to attribute has been deprecated. Use the model attribute instead.
```

Regards,
Lorcan

issue_title: Don't release the solver just to refresh constraints; remove variable parsing hacks
issue_body:
There are several hacks in mechaphish involving parsing the variable name to extract metadata about a variable. It's 2018 now, and we have the variable tracking mechanism in the solver, which works _great_. However, it can't be used in these same mechaphish context because there's a bad habit of running `state.release_plugin('solver')` whenever we need to tweak anything about the state's constraints, thus also discarding the variable metadata.

Is this really the only way to discard all cached remenants of the old constraints? Can we add actual interfaces to the solver plugin to remove constraints and invalidate caches?

issue_title: Is division signed or unsigned ?
issue_body:
If I create these variables :
a = claripy.BVV(5, 8)
b = claripy.BVV(254, 8)

Then, "a / b" is zero. So it's like the division is unsigned.

However, if now I set b to be a claripy.BVS('denominator', 8)
Then I instantiate a claripy.Solver, add a constraint that "a / b > 10" and that "b > 0", it is satisfiable, and it tells me that it works with b being 254.

So, I suppose this is not coherent.
Maybe you could make it more explicit, and you could also support both signed/unsigned divisions ?

Thanks !

```

#### Problem description

Digging through the source code, the problem seems to be with the use of `np.str` https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/blob/99c2f7269a40cb17ea661cb25db419e48b57d105/satpy/readers/native_msg_hdr.py#L170.

`np.str` seems to make data be read in `utf-32`:

```python
data = b'some data'
dt = np.dtype((np.str, 1))
print(np_str_dt.itemsize)
# 4
np.frombuffer(data, dtype=dt, count=1)
# raises same error: 
# UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-32-le' codec can't decode bytes in position 0-3: code point not in range(0x110000)
```
replacing with `'|S{num_bytes}'` and things work as expected:
```python
data = b'some data'
dt = np.dtype('|S1')
dt.itemsize
# 1
np.frombuffer(data, dtype=dt, count=1)
# array([b's'], dtype='|S1')
```

#### Versions of Python, package at hand and relevant dependencies
Python 3.6, satpy 0.8.1

issue_title: rqalpha中关于Portfolio和Account的逻辑问题。
issue_body:
在rqalpha中, 一个Portfolio下可以包含多个Account,  而且可以包含多个不同类型的帐号, 也就是可以同时包含股票和期货等不同类型的帐号,  这样不但导致设计复杂，而且逻辑上与现实是不符合的.

在现实中, 股票和期货是在不同的地方开户的,  他们交易的官方机构不同，交易对象的性质不同, 把他们放在一起做投资组合进行量化分析是没有意义的。

股票有很多因子, 做量化分析就是希望能找出合理的量化策略来运用这些因子进行买卖, 但如果把股票的因子运行在期货上是行不通的.

在同一个策略里同时购买股票和期货是没有任何意义的,  进行回测也是没有意义的, 找不出有任何有价值的因子或策略.

总之，设计应该尽量与现实保持一致, 而不要过渡设计.

issue_title: add WN dir to PATH
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm constantly hitting this as a common thing (hence PR such as the one against the Localhost which was ultimately dropped)

Do we have a policy about whether the WN dir where a command is running from should be in the PATH or not?

We already change the env such that we pick up supporting code we ship with jobs to some backends.

Would it be sensible to say that it's a logical extension to include the dir of the WN to the PATH of a job?
(and possibly the LD_LIBRARY_PATH?)

This is would require a relatively minor PR but would remove the amount of times I've been tripped up by having to do this by hand in a job I'm executing.

Does anyone have any thoughts on this?

Rob

issue_title: Hunting race conditions in the memory backend
issue_body:
Here is a script called `race.py`.

```python
import aiohttp
import asyncio
import async_timeout
import base64

AUTH = 'Basic ' + base64.b64encode(b'test:test').decode('utf-8')
URL = 'http://localhost:8888/v1/buckets/default/collections/plop'


async def create_collection(session):
    async with session.put(URL, data='{"data": {"id": "plop"}}', headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"}) as response:
        text = await response.text()
        print(text)


async def set_data(session):
    with async_timeout.timeout(10):
        async with session.put(URL, data='{"data": {"a": 1}}', headers=[("Content-Type", "application/json")]) as response:
            text = await response.text()
            print(text)
            if response.status == 409:
                print(response.headers)


async def set_permission(session):
    with async_timeout.timeout(10):
        async with session.put(URL, data='{"permissions": {"read": ["github:n1k0"]}}', headers=[("Content-Type", "application/json")]) as response:
            text = await response.text()
            print(text)
            if response.status == 409:
                print(response.headers)


async def fetch(session):
    with async_timeout.timeout(10):
        async with session.get(URL) as response:
            resp = await response.json()
            print(resp)
            assert resp['data'].get('a') == 1


async def remove_bucket(session):
    async with session.delete('http://localhost:8888/v1/buckets/default') as response:
        text = await response.text()
        print(text)
        if response.status == 409:
            print(response.headers)

async def remove_permission(session):
    async with session.patch(URL, data='[ { "op": "remove", "path": "/permissions/read/github:n1k0" } ]', headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json-patch+json'}) as response:
        return await response.json()


async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(headers={"Authorization": AUTH}) as session1:
        await remove_bucket(session1)
        #await create_collection(session1)    # see (2)
        async with aiohttp.ClientSession(headers={"Authorization": AUTH}) as session2:
            await asyncio.gather(set_permission(session2), set_data(session1))
            pass
        await fetch(session1)

        resp1 = await remove_permission(session1)
        print(resp1)
        assert resp1['data'].get('a') == 1
        assert 'read' not in resp1['permissions']

        await fetch(session1)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

It tries to make two requests "simultaneously", one which sets a collection's data, and another which adds a permission. Then it checks to see that the data is correct.

Run kinto using the tests/kinto.ini config from kinto-http.js. Then, run the above script using a command like (fish shell syntax):

```
set x 0; while python race.py ; set x (math $x + 1); date; echo $x; end
```

On my machine, the script eventually fails (although sometimes it takes more time -- up to 2k iterations). Either the `set_data` or the `set_permission` call gets a 409 integrity error.

```
{"message":"Integrity constraint violated, please retry.","code":409,"errno":122,"error":"Conflict"}
{"data":{"a":1,"id":"plop","last_modified":1498756145457},"permissions":{"write":["basicauth:c5f27b9d5865fee5a3df09402958c11146286ec9c0599207d3ca4c2715a28056"]}}
{'data': {'a': 1, 'last_modified': 1498756145457, 'id': 'plop'}, 'permissions': {'write': ['basicauth:c5f27b9d5865fee5a3df09402958c11146286ec9c0599207d3ca4c2715a28056']}}
{'errno': 107, 'error': 'Invalid parameters', 'code': 400, 'message': "body: JSON Patch operation failed: can't remove non-existent object 'github:n1k0'", 'details': [{'location': 'body', 'description': "JSON Patch operation failed: can't remove non-existent object 'github:n1k0'", 'name': None}]}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "race-trash.py", line 59, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 387, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 274, in result
    raise self._exception
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "race-trash.py", line 52, in main
    assert resp1['data'].get('a') == 1
KeyError: 'data'
```


```
{"data":{"id":"plop","last_modified":1498756212749},"permissions":{"read":["github:n1k0"],"write":["basicauth:c5f27b9d5865fee5a3df09402958c11146286ec9c0599207d3ca4c2715a28056"]}}
{"message":"Integrity constraint violated, please retry.","code":409,"errno":122,"error":"Conflict"}
{'data': {'id': 'plop', 'last_modified': 1498756212749}, 'permissions': {'read': ['github:n1k0'], 'write': ['basicauth:c5f27b9d5865fee5a3df09402958c11146286ec9c0599207d3ca4c2715a28056']}}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "race-trash.py", line 59, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 387, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 274, in result
    raise self._exception
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "race-trash.py", line 48, in main
    await fetch(session1)
  File "race-trash.py", line 29, in fetch
    assert resp['data'].get('a') == 1
AssertionError
```

I believe these correspond to the intermittent failures we've seen on kinto-http.js tests.

I was surprised for these to get 409 integrity errors because the memory backend is not transactional. However, what happens is in `UserResource`; a `put` fetches the record to apply 412 "If-Match"/"If-None-Match" checks, and then uses that fetched record to either `create` or `update`. If two requests happen at the same time, they can both try to do a `create`; one will "win", and the other will discover that the record already exists, so raise an `IntegrityError`.

1. Is this OK? Should a client be able to expect not to get an IntegrityError in these kinds of circumstances? We are correctly reporting an IntegrityError with a Retry-After, so arguably this is all working as designed.
2. However, if the record already exists (by uncommenting the line marked "see (2)"), it's possible for the two "updates" to race with the loser not getting saved. Is this OK? The memory backend doesn't have any kind of transactional guarantees and it's essentially only for testing.

issue_title: user-friendly error messages regarding discrete vs. continuous wavelets in transform routines
issue_body:
The following example output from #330 demonstrates the message given by wavedec if a `ContinousWavelet` is passed in.
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
data = pywt.wavedec(S, wavelet)
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pywt/_multilevel.py", line 87, in wavedec
wavelet = Wavelet(wavelet)
File "pywt/_extensions/_pywt.pyx", line 335, in pywt._extensions._pywt.Wavelet.cinit (pywt/_extensions/_pywt.c:6082)
AttributeError: 'pywt._extensions._pywt.ContinuousWavelet' object has no attribute 'lower'
```

It would be more helpful to inform the user directly that ContinousWavelets are not supported by this transform and a discrete `pywt.Wavelet` should be used instead. 

Although the example above is for `wavedec` it is likely a similar problem for the other transforms.

issue_title: Recent commit breaks mujoco-py on Ubuntu Trusty
issue_body:
While I am still able to run mujoco-py on my Ubuntu Xenial 16.04 LTS laptop, a recent change has broken my Travis builds, which install mujoco-py on their most recent linux image, Ubuntu Trusty 12.04:

    untravis@travis-job-32e4033d-6e2e-4521-a1a9-ed876b16ba75:~/build/vlad17/cmpc$ uname -a
    Linux travis-job-32e4033d-6e2e-4521-a1a9-ed876b16ba75 4.4.0-101-generic #124~14.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Fri Nov 10 19:05:36 UTC 2017 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

On the travis trusty image above, after installing all requisite dependencies and updating the load path

    sudo apt-get -qq update
    sudo apt-get install -y \
         cmake zlib1g-dev libjpeg-dev xvfb libav-tools \
         xorg-dev libboost-all-dev swig libosmesa6-dev libglew-dev
    sudo apt-get install -y libsdl2-dev || sudo apt-get install -f
    export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$HOME/.mujoco/mjpro150/bin
    export LIBRARY_PATH=$HOME/.mujoco/mjpro150/bin

On this machine, and I presume all trusty machines, version `1.50.1.38` installs successfully while `1.50.1.39` does not [full install trace](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/files/1713742/install-trace.txt):

    (python3.5.5) travis@travis-job-32e4033d-6e2e-4521-a1a9-ed876b16ba75:~/build/vlad17/cmpc$ pip install mujoco_py==1.50.1.38
    ...
    Successfully installed mujoco-py-1.50.1.38
    (python3.5.5) travis@travis-job-32e4033d-6e2e-4521-a1a9-ed876b16ba75:~/build/vlad17/cmpc$ pip install mujoco_py==1.50.1.39
    ...
        ImportError: libOSMesa.so.8: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
        
        ----------------------------------------
      Rolling back uninstall of mujoco-py
    Command "/home/travis/virtualenv/python3.5.5/bin/python -u -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-cokl6y7h/mujoco-py/setup.py';f=getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__);code=f.read().replace('\r\n', '\n');f.close();exec(compile(code, __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-wd7a6rl9-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /home/travis/virtualenv/python3.5.5/include/site/python3.5/mujoco-py" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-cokl6y7h/mujoco-py/

Following the error message:

    (python3.5.5) travis@travis-job-32e4033d-6e2e-4521-a1a9-ed876b16ba75:~/build/vlad17/cmpc$ echo /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libOSMesa*
    /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libOSMesa.so /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libOSMesa.so.6 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libOSMesa.so.6.5.3

Indeed, the trusty `libosmesa6-dev` package [does not provide libOSMesa.so.8](https://packages.ubuntu.com/trusty/amd64/libosmesa6/filelist), unlike its xenial counterpart. Why is the shift to `*.so.8` necessary? Can it be changed back to `6` so that older linuxes can still use mujoco-py?

issue_title: Mutations now have no access to context, resolve methods broken
issue_body:
The context story seems very confused in the newest version. My understanding from [the graphql-js 0.5.0 release notes](https://github.com/graphql/graphql-js/releases/tag/v0.5.0) is that all resolving functions should now have the signature `([self,] args, context, info)`, as opposed to `([self,] args, info)`.

At the moment, if I update a `resolve_NAME` method to take context, then it throws argument errors.

Also, mutations don't take context and therefore they cannot access context data. It think a patch similar to [this one](https://github.com/graphql/graphql-relay-js/pull/77) on graphql-relay-js is necessary for the relay mutations at least, but probably for all of them.

issue_title: Option for strict key ordering in results.
issue_body:
Related to #18, sometimes users might want their results to be in the same order as they are in the fields. In this case, I am thinking that it should be an optional argument (default False) to the `Executor`.

In the default, it will use regular `dict` objects to store results. But if say `enforce_strict_ordering=True` is passed to the Executor, then we will use `OrderedDict` instead.

```
EMBEDDED = True
if EMBEDDED:
    from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, Dense
    from tensorflow.keras.models import Model
else:
    from keras.layers import Input, Dense
    from keras.models import Model

inp = Input((32,))
x = Dense(1)(inp)
model = Model(inp, x)
model.name = 'mymodel'
```
Setting EMBEDDED to False it uses the independent Keras library, and it works.

issue_title: Implement reattach process using stored dataset
issue_body:
The active dataset and pending dataset could be stored in non-volatile memory with #866, then we need to implement reattach process after reset, and it will help to pass certification test 9.2.8, 9.2.15, 9.2.16.

The detailed reattach process defined in the Thread specification:

> In cases where a Thread device loses its Delay Timer state (e.g. due to a reset), the Thread device MUST first attempt to reattach using the Active Operational Dataset stored in non-volatile memory. If the Thread device fails to find a Parent using the Active Operational Dataset, the Thread device MUST attempt to reattach using the Pending Operational Dataset stored in non-volatile memory. If the Thread device fails to find a Parent using the Pending Operational Dataset, the Thread device MUST revert back to using the Active Operational Dataset and start a new Delay Timer with a conservative value that ensures expiration at or after the expected time. For example, a device that only has knowledge of the Delay Timer value when it received the Pending Operational Dataset MUST start the Delay Timer using the Delay Timer value it originally received.

this is something related to issue #600 however we raise a new issue to separately track this.

I'd propose to simplify things by adding a user with username `enable` and password with `enable secret`, without modifying base api. 

Or another way is like @mirceaulinic suggested to add a new flag 'system_users' and add `enable secret` in `ios`, `admin` in `eos` and `junos`, `root` in `junos` as a list to it. This will require more work to change base api but still a valid option.

looking forward to @ktbyers @dbarrosop @mirceaulinic  further comment.

issue_title: Windows troubles with default program to open .py files
issue_body:
On my Windows 10 virtual machine, I set Atom as the default program to open .py files.  This was a big mistake, and now when I try to run PmagPy programs, Atom is opened.  I tried resetting it using "choose default apps" for .py files, but there is no appropriate python option available that way.  I suspect I would have to go into the registry and select python.exe, or something like that.  

This stack overflow question gets at the same thing: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/15121714/how-do-i-get-a-python-program-to-run-instead-of-opening-in-notepad

This seems like a problem a user easily might encounter, too.  I can just spin up a new virtual machine, but I am stumped on how to simply fix this, which we would want a user to be able to do.  

Anyone with more Windows savvy have thoughts on this?  @Caoimhinmg, @rminnett?

issue_title: Ruby gRPC client generation should be merged into gapic pipeline
issue_body:
The gcloud-ruby project actually bundles gRPC generated files as a part of the google-cloud libraries (for example, gcloud-ruby/google-cloud-pubsub contains the gRPC files at https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-ruby/tree/master/google-cloud-pubsub/lib/google/pubsub/v1).

For non-cloud APIs, I think either design (i.e. merging into a single package or separating Gapic client from gRPC client) would be okay. So because of this gcloud-ruby design, I think all Ruby's generated artifacts will follow this style.

The current design of Ruby's grpc client generation pipeline doesn't fit with this design. It should:
- [ ] stop using packman but use the protoc command directly
- [ ] introduce this protoc task as a part of Ruby's Gapic pipeline -- so that the generated files reside in a same package

`def roc_auc_score(y_pred, y_true):

    Yan, L., Dodier, R., Mozer, M. C., & Wolniewicz, R. (2003).
    Optimizing Classifier Performance via an Approximation to the Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney Statistic.
    Measures overall performance for a full range of threshold levels.
    Arguments:
        y_pred: `Tensor`. Predicted values.
        y_true: `Tensor` . Targets (labels), a probability distribution.
    """
    with tf.name_scope("RocAucScore"):
        pos = tf.boolean_mask(y_pred, tf.cast(y_true, tf.bool))
        neg = tf.boolean_mask(y_pred, ~tf.cast(y_true, tf.bool))
        pos = tf.expand_dims(pos, 0)
        neg = tf.expand_dims(neg, 1)
        # original paper suggests performance is robust to exact parameter choice
        gamma = 0.2
        p     = 3
        difference = tf.zeros_like(pos * neg) + pos - neg - gamma
        masked = tf.boolean_mask(difference, difference < 0.0)
        return tf.reduce_sum(tf.pow(-masked, p))`

issue_title: "RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object" when using tflearn Trainer
issue_body:
I followed the [example](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/extending_tensorflow/trainer.py) in using TFLearn's Trainer class to be able to train your own models not covered by TFLearn. Thus me having this code:

```
import dataset_utils
import tensorflow as tf
import tflearn

from tensorflow.contrib import grid_rnn


def main(_):
    image_paths, labels = dataset_utils.read_dataset_list('../test/dummy_labels_file.txt')
    data_dir = "../test/dummy_data/"
    images = dataset_utils.read_images(data_dir=data_dir, image_paths=image_paths, image_extension='png')
    print('Done reading images')
    images = dataset_utils.resize(images, (1596, 48))
    images = dataset_utils.transpose(images)
    labels = dataset_utils.encode(labels)
    x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = dataset_utils.split(features=images, test_size=0.5, labels=labels)
    y_train = dataset_utils.convert_to_sparse(y_train)
    y_test = dataset_utils.convert_to_sparse(y_test)

    with tf.Graph().as_default():
        X = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, None, 48])
        Y = tf.sparse_placeholder(tf.int32)
        seq_lens = tf.placeholder(tf.int32, [None])

        def dnn(x):
            cell_fw = grid_rnn.Grid2LSTMCell(num_units=128)
            cell_bw = grid_rnn.Grid2LSTMCell(num_units=128)
            bidirectional_grid_rnn = tf.nn.bidirectional_dynamic_rnn(cell_fw, cell_bw, x, dtype=tf.float32)
            outputs = tf.reshape(bidirectional_grid_rnn[0], [-1, 256])

            W = tf.Variable(tf.truncated_normal([256,
                                                 80],
                                                stddev=0.1, dtype=tf.float32), name='W')
            b = tf.Variable(tf.constant(0., dtype=tf.float32, shape=[80], name='b'))

            logits = tf.matmul(outputs, W) + b
            logits = tf.reshape(logits, [tf.shape(x)[0], -1, 80])
            logits = tf.transpose(logits, (1, 0, 2))
            return logits

        net = dnn(X)
        decoded, _ = tf.nn.ctc_beam_search_decoder(net, seq_lens, merge_repeated=False)
        cost = tf.reduce_mean(tf.nn.ctc_loss(inputs=net, labels=Y, sequence_length=seq_lens))
        optimizer = tf.train.MomentumOptimizer(learning_rate=0.001, momentum=0.5)
        label_error_rate = tf.reduce_mean(tf.edit_distance(tf.cast(decoded[0], tf.int32), Y))

        train_op = tflearn.TrainOp(loss=cost, optimizer=optimizer, metric=label_error_rate, batch_size=1)
        trainer = tflearn.Trainer(train_ops=train_op, tensorboard_verbose=0)

        trainer.fit({X: x_train, Y: y_train, seq_lens: dataset_utils.get_seq_lens(x_train)},
                    val_feed_dicts={X: x_test, Y: y_test, seq_lens: dataset_utils.get_seq_lens(x_test)},
                    n_epoch=1,
                    show_metric=True)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    tf.app.run(main=main)
```

Unfortunately, when I run the code, it throws a `RecursionError` and I want to figure out how to fix it. To reproduce it, simply clone this [repository](https://github.com/selcouthlyBlue/simplified_bi_lstm_ocr) run train_using_tflearn_trainer.py.

issue_title: Not passing a protected header when creating a compact JWE causes KeyError
issue_body:
Minimal test case:

```python
# testcase.py
from jwcrypto.jwe import JWE
from jwcrypto.jwk import JWK

payload = "some payload text"

jwe = JWE(payload)

key = JWK.generate(kty='RSA', size=4096)

header = {
    "alg": "RSA-OAEP-256",
    "enc": "A256CBC-HS512",
    "typ": "JWE",
}

jwe.add_recipient(key, header)

serialised = jwe.serialize(compact=True)
```

```
$ python testcase.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "testcase.py", line 18, in <module>
    serialised = jwe.serialize(compact=True)
  File "/home/michael/code/jwcrypto/jwcrypto/jwe.py", line 285, in serialize
    ph = json_decode(self.objects['protected'])
KeyError: 'protected'
```

It seems like this is a fairly easy fix to initialise the 'protected' key in 'objects' to `{}`, but I'm not sure if this is allowed by the JWE spec?

issue_title: Document how to use new Mailgun EU region
issue_body:
Mailgun [has introduced](https://www.mailgun.com/blog/we-have-a-new-region-in-europe-yall) a new European region. Using it with Anymail should be as simple as providing the EU endpoint in Anymail's [`MAILGUN_API_URL`](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/mailgun/#std:setting-ANYMAIL_MAILGUN_API_URL) setting:

```python
ANYMAIL = {
    "MAILGUN_API_KEY": "...",
    "MAILGUN_API_URL": "https://api.eu.mailgun.net/v3",
    # ...
}
```

Should update the Anymail docs to include this info.

issue_title: Deployment.ready may not accurately reflect deployment status
issue_body:
The check `self.obj["status"]["updatedReplicas"] == self.replicas` does not necessarily indicate the deployment is successful.  It probably works ok if you are doing a `create()` but not if you are doing an `update()`.  For instance if you have 2 replicas and update the deployment with a surge of 2 you will see an `updatedReplicas` of 2 but you will also see `unavailableReplicas` at 2.  Following is an example of updating a deployment in this scenario.  The `True` at the end of the line is the `ready` value.

```
2016-10-04 13:33:07,436 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 2, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 3} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:09,535 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:11,632 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:13,730 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:15,824 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:17,922 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:20,017 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:22,115 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'unavailableReplicas': 1, u'availableReplicas': 1, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
2016-10-04 13:33:24,212 - root - INFO - {u'observedGeneration': 15, u'updatedReplicas': 2, u'availableReplicas': 2, u'replicas': 2} 5 True
```

I'm doing this right now but it is ugly.  Would love for a cleaner check.

```
    start_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    while (datetime.datetime.now() - start_time).total_seconds() < timeout:
        try:
            deployment.reload()
            current_revision = deployment.annotations['deployment.kubernetes.io/revision']
            logging.info("%s %s", deployment.obj['status'], current_revision)

            if int(current_revision) < int(our_revision):
                logging.info("Waiting for our deployment to start")
                time.sleep(2)
                continue

            if 'availableReplicas' in deployment.obj['status'] and 'updatedReplicas' in deployment.obj['status'] and \
                deployment.obj['status']['updatedReplicas'] == deployment.obj['status']['availableReplicas'] and \
                deployment.obj['status']['replicas'] == deployment.obj['status']['availableReplicas']:

                #Final check, just in case
                if 'unavailableReplicas' in deployment.obj['status'] and \
                    deployment.obj['status']['unavailableReplicas'] != 0:
                    logging.info("Unavailable != 0")
                    time.sleep(2)
                    continue

                logging.info("Done waiting for deployment")
                if current_revision != our_revision:
                    logging.info("Looks like our deployment got bumped ours: %s current: %s", \
                                 our_revision, current_revision)
                return current_revision

        except Exception:  #pylint: disable=w0703
            logging.error("Error:", exc_info=True)
```

Issue with boulder-start.sh
issue_body:
When I run boulder start on master:
```
(venv)➜  ~/github/letsencrypt git:(master) ./tests/boulder-start.sh
+ git clone -b docker-integration https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder
Cloning into 'boulder'...
remote: Counting objects: 23370, done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (88/88), done.
remote: Total 23370 (delta 40), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 23280
Receiving objects: 100% (23370/23370), 18.37 MiB | 452.00 KiB/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (14269/14269), done.
Checking connectivity... done.
+ cd
+ sed -i 's/FAKE_DNS: .*/FAKE_DNS: 172.17.42.1/' docker-compose.yml
sed: can't read docker-compose.yml: No such file or directory
./tests/boulder-start.sh: line 9: ./start.py: No such file or directory
```
The problem:
1. `boulder-start.sh` [calls](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/tests/boulder-start.sh#L6) the `boulder-fetch.sh` script. 
2. `boulder-fetch.sh` tries to git clone boulder into the `$BOULDERPATh` directory [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/tests/boulder-fetch.sh#L6). But that isn't set anywhere. So it just it just goes in the certbot directory.
3. We pop back to `boulder-start.sh`, where we `cd $GOPATH/src/github.com/letsencrypt/boulder` because this used to be where boulder would go, back when we got boulder with `go get`. So this directory either doesn't exist or is empty (my case), or has an old copy of boulder.
4. Try to run `start.py`, but it isn't there so fail.

Since we need to get a special branch, I don't think we could use go get. So we need to set `$BOULDERPATH` properly. Probably to `$GOPATH/src/github.com/letsencrypt/` in `boulder-start.sh`.

issue_title: 2.4.1 breaks unpredictably
issue_body:
Because 2.4.1 introduces a file named `types.py` and the framework generally relies on relative imports, depending on how `api_config.py` is imported, `import types as endpoints_types` may import `endpoints.types` or it may import `types`, the Python standard library.

We should switch to absolute imports as soon as is feasible and use the `from __future__ import absolute_import` directive.

In the meantime, we can issue a quick fix by renaming `types.py` to `endpoints_types.py`

issue_title: Use a state specific replay attack mitigation scheme
issue_body:
With #287 we implement a replay attack mitigation scheme, that relies on the state of one (and only one) raiden network. We assume, that a channel will have a limited number of messages per lifetime and apply a starting nonce scheme, based on the block number.

Since anyone can start a new raiden network by just deploying another instance of the `Registry` contract, there is no guarantee that Alice and Bob will never have a channel for the same asset opened at the same block number in two raiden networks `R` and `R'` (which would lead to the same nonce ranges being valid in channel `AB(R)` and `AB(R')`).

A state specific replay attack mitigation scheme would (instead of relying on the opening block number) use something specific to the channel contract instance, e.g. hashing the channel contract address into each signature.

Note, that this potentially still leaves replay attack vectors open for raiden instances on different blockchains (i.e. testnet vs mainnet), across forks, etc...

For further hardening, the opening party could provide a state specific piece of information (e.g. genesis block hash/fork block hash) that would be concatenated with the channel contract address for signatures.

Whether we need this second level of replay protection should be discussed.

issue_title: Synchronize API calls that use non-constant smart contract functions
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current API functions that call non-constant smart contract functions need synchronization, this is required to avoid sending transactions for the same effect.

A user might make two or more HTTP requests with the same token and partner, since only the first transaction can succeed the additional ones will only burn gas.

## Solution

stateful functions exposed with the current REST API draft

-    get_new_events
-   open
-    deposit
-    close
-    settle

stateful functions not exposed with the current REST API draft

-    transfer
-    transfer_and_wait
-    transfer_async

Note: Even if these functions are exposed in the future, we don't need to control the concurrent usage.

## TODO

semantics for the concurrency control

-  linearize the calls:
    - synchronized for the same cursor
        - get_new_events
- prohibit concurrent calls
    - channel manager with the same partner address
        - open
    - on the channel
        - deposit, close, and settle

issue_title: Feature Request: Convert tspan to text elements for SVG tiny

Hi Jeff,

I am using the qtsoftware SVG renderer to display Inkscape generated SVG content. I am using your scour tool to clean up the Inkscape files before importing.

issue_title: Closed contour issue
issue_body:
I took a look at issue https://github.com/pytrip/pytripgui/issues/72 and found the source of the error.

Issue is, whether the last point in a contour should be repeated at PyTRiP level or not.
Observations:
- **Old VDX format:** repeats last point to close contour
- **New VDX format:** repeats last point to close contour
- **DICOM:** does **not** repeat last point

I would like to have consistent behaviour, so either we repeat the last point in the contour object or not, no matter how the original contour data were loaded from VDX or DICOM.
So:
1) choose to repeat last point at PyTRiP Contour() level:
  -- requires slight modification of dicom read/write
2) choose **not** to repeat last point at PyTRiP Contour() level:
  -- requires slight adjustment of VDX readers and writers
  -- requires slight adjustment of PyTRiPGUI

I might even prefer 2) just to avoid messing around with DICOM and from the philosophy of staying close to DICOM if possible.

Also, I think it would give nicer code when implementing POIs (see https://github.com/pytrip/pytripgui/issues/63 and #314 )

issue_title: Incorrectly identified OS when building on amazonlinux docker
issue_body:
Building `thinc` eventually results in the following error:

```
building 'thinc.openblas' extension
  gcc -pthread -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -fPIC -I/usr/local/include/python3.6m -I/tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/include -I/tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/thinc/_files -I/usr/local/include/python3.6m -c thinc/openblas.cpp -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6/thinc/openblas.o -O2 -Wno-strict-prototypes -Wno-unused-function
  cc1plus: warning: command line option '-Wno-strict-prototypes' is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++ [enabled by default]
  In file included from /tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/thinc/_files/cblas.h:5:0,
                   from thinc/openblas.cpp:579:
  /tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/thinc/_files/common_x86_64.h: In function 'void blas_lock(volatile BLASULONG*)':
  /tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/thinc/_files/common.h:359:30: error: 'sched_yield' was not declared in this scope
   #define YIELDING sched_yield()
                                ^
  /tmp/pip-install-c8xrng96/thinc/thinc/_files/common_x86_64.h:75:23: note: in expansion of macro 'YIELDING'
       while (*address) {YIELDING;};
                         ^
  error: command 'gcc' failed with exit status 1
```

By doing some digging, I've identified that this error is caused by lacking 
```
#include <sched.h>
```
which is skipped because neither of these conditions are true:

```
#ifdef OS_LINUX
#include <malloc.h>
#include <sched.h>
#endif

#if defined(OS_DARWIN) || defined(OS_FREEBSD) || defined(OS_NETBSD) || defined(OS_ANDROID)
#include <sched.h>
#endif
```

By faking `OS_LINUX` definition, it compiles fine.
Problem is, that it's a bit harder to fake this flag when installing from pip

issue_title: Execution graph of Make style in nested workflow with `sos_run`
issue_body:
I find it useful to have some "template" step that does not have any input / output files specified when they are defined;  but will take later those values from elsewhere and execute. That way a "template" can be used by many other steps without having to repeat itself. Currently the only way seems to be using `sos_run` and I have a toy example here that attempts to achieve this:

```
[template1]
parameter: output_files = list
output: output_files, group_by = 1
task:
bash:
  sleep 1
  touch ${_output}

[template2]
parameter: input_files = list
parameter: output_files = list
input: input_files, group_by = 1
output: output_files, group_by = 1
task:
bash:
  sleep 1
  echo ${_input} ${_output}

[main1: provides = "1.txt"]
output: "1.txt"
sos_run("template1", output_files = ["1.txt"])

[main2: provides = "2.txt"]
depends: "1.txt" 
output: "2.txt"
sos_run("template2", input_files = ["1.txt"], output_files = ["2.txt"])

[main3: provides = "3.txt"]
output: "3.txt"
sos_run("template1", output_files = ["3.txt"])

[main4: provides = "4.txt"]
depends: "3.txt" 
output: "4.txt"
sos_run("template2", input_files = ["3.txt"], output_files = ["4.txt"])

[default]
depends : "2.txt", "4.txt"
```

First, this script does not work ... I cannot figure out why it went wrong. But this is not my point. What I'm actually concerned is that it does not seem to generate a proper DAG. If I do not use the nested workflow idea and write it like:

```
[main1: provides = ["1.txt"]]
parameter: output_files = ["1.txt" ]
output: output_files, group_by = 1
task:
bash:
  sleep 1
  touch ${_output}

[main2: provides = ["2.txt"]]
parameter: input_files = ["1.txt"]
parameter: output_files = ["2.txt"]
depends: input_files 
input: input_files, group_by = 1

issue_title: Use pandas.read_sas instead of sas7bdat
issue_body: Pandas has this feature so we should not rely on sas7bdat. We would have to make sure pandas is recent enough though.

issue_title: Display side panel by default.
issue_body: It might be a good idea to display side panel by default, at least as a way to make users aware of this feature.

issue_title: Random identical tasks.
issue_body: In many cases, especially for simulation studies, there is a need to run identical tasks repeatedly. I would recommend that each instance is associated with a RNG seed so that they can be separated and executed as separate tasks. I am however wondering if we should have internal support for truly random executions (e.g. generate a random number for task id).

issue_title: Remote execution of workflows / auxiliary steps.
issue_body:
It is even conceptually clear what sos should do for the execution of workflows remotely. The problem is that if we call the workflow with `sos_run`, it would trigger other steps, which might or might not use remote targets. Also, it is unclear how to trigger auxiliary step from a remote step ...

The bottom line is that our current remote execution model is limited to single tasks, not workflows.

issue_title: Sort tasks by time created in the output of sos status.
issue_body: This would keep batches of tasks together and help us identify new tasks.

issue_title: Backend authenticate kwargs ignored
issue_body:
The kwargs passed to `BaseAuth.authenticate()` are ignored: https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/c03e669a1f98f03e9a838ce3a798a25ad53e9455/social_core/backends/base.py#L73-L74. This is specifically a problem for my team in Django. At this point in the code `self.strategy.request` is `None`. However, the strategy passed in as a kwarg is the instance that has been used for much of the process prior to the call to `authenticate`. `kwargs['strategy'].request` contains the request made to the server to complete authentication.

This is important for my team because we rely on the request for multi-tenancy. We pull the site/tenant from the strategy's request, and from there get the associated configuration: https://github.com/edx/ecommerce/blob/master/ecommerce/social_auth/strategies.py#L12.

I propose modifying

```python
self.strategy = self.strategy or kwargs.get('strategy')
self.redirect_uri = self.redirect_uri or kwargs.get('redirect_uri')
```

to

```python
self.strategy = kwargs.get('strategy', self.strategy)
self.redirect_uri = kwargs.get('redirect_uri', self.redirect_uri)
```

issue_title: JSON Export crashes if no list annotations
Debug lines starting with certain comment
Wrong endpoint used in metadata when specifying viewset
issue_body:
Hi,
When an endpoint is registered with a specific viewset, a different endpoint is generated in determine_metadata (hence losing all out custom attributes). The responsible part in metada.py:
```
        try:
            serializer_instance = view.get_serializer()
        except Exception:
            # Custom viewset is expecting something we can't guess
            serializer_instance = view.get_serializer_class()()
        endpoint = None
        if hasattr(view, 'endpoint'):
            endpoint = view.endpoint
        else:
            if hasattr(view.get_serializer_class().Meta, 'model'):
                from .endpoints import Endpoint
                endpoint = Endpoint(view.get_serializer_class().Meta.model, viewset=view)
```
The endpoint relation is normally set by the viewset_factory, and it's not when specifying a viewset. 
Came up with this, not ideal, fix :
```
class Endpoint(with_metaclass(EndpointMetaClass, BaseEndpoint)):

    def __init__(self, model=None, **kwargs):
        self.inflector = Inflector(self.inflector_language)

        if model is not None:
            self.model = model

        arg_names = ('fields', 'serializer', 'permission_classes', 'filter_fields', 'search_fields',
                     'viewset', 'read_only', 'include_str', 'ordering_fields', 'page_size',
                     'base_viewset', 'fields_annotation', 'fieldsets', 'base_serializer', 'list_me')
        for arg_name in arg_names:
            setattr(self, arg_name, kwargs.pop(arg_name, getattr(self, arg_name, None)))
            if arg_name == 'viewset' and getattr(self, arg_name):
                assert getattr(self.viewset, 'endpoint', None) is None, 'This viewset is already registered by an endpoint'
                setattr(self.viewset, 'endpoint', self)
```
I think we have to prevent the same viewset to be registered by different endpoints or the metadata woulnd't mean anything. We could also test the class instead of the objects.. (The tests will not pass as the same viewset is registered multiple times for now)

issue_title: predict np.vstack problem when batch_size if no factor of test dataset size
issue_body:
Have a trained network. The test set includes 28000 rows. If I set the batch_size to `256`, i.e.,

`prediction = tl.utils.predict(sess, network, test_X, X, y_op, batch_size=256)
`

the following exception is thrown:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:/Project/Python/kaggle/mnist/run.py", line 136, in <module>
    prediction = tl.utils.predict(sess, network, test_X, X, y_op, batch_size=256)
  File "D:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\tensorlayer\utils.py", line 296, in predict
    result = np.vstack((result, result_a))
  File "D:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\numpy\core\shape_base.py", line 234, in vstack
    return _nx.concatenate([atleast_2d(_m) for _m in tup], 0)
ValueError: all the input array dimensions except for the concatenation axis must match exactly
```

If set the `batch_size` to a factor of `28000`, like `400`. the problem goes away.

Looking into the `utils.py`, the exception is caused by the fact that `tl.utils.predict` tries to vstack the results from each batch. In my case, `predict` tries to divide the 28000 results into groups of 256, which left 96 extra results. When stacking the 96 results which has shape `[1,96]` with the other results with shape `[109,256]', the issue happens.

I think this issue this is important because some time the number test set is prime number.

issue_title: 图片对不同分辨率的手机适应性欠佳，希望加入SIFT算法
Using a smaller k in classification
issue_body:
Even though it's not recommended, it might be possible to use ProPhyle with a smaller k. Two things can then go wrong:
* non-propagating a k-mer
* non-propagating and propagating a k-mer at the same time (so it would be effectively counted multiple times) - thanks @simonepignotti for reporting

We should decide whether we will support smaller ks.

issue_title: move linux test from semaphore ci to circie
issue_body:
Circle CI 的优势在于：

* 可以并行四个容器
* 配置文件是 yml 的，所以可以纳入版本管理，也可以在不同 branch 下使用不同的配置
* 在 PR 页面可以每个 job 一条 status ，更加直观
* Docker-based workflow，可以更加方便的控制 Session 相关程序的版本依赖
